source: Autonomous web research by Chief Decifer
synthesis: The 2023–2025 research consensus is unambiguous: static Kelly sizing is the most dangerous thing a systematic equity bot can do. Full Kelly consistently over-bets due to parameter estimation error, and the literature's central recommendation is to treat the raw Kelly output as an upper-bound cap, not a target, then layer on fractional scaling, volatility-targeting, and drawdown-aware position reduction. Volatility targeting — scaling exposure inversely to realised vol — empirically improves Sharpe ratios for equity strategies by introducing a natural short-term momentum overlay (the 'leverage effect') and reduces left-tail severity. CVaR dominates VaR as a coherent portfolio risk measure because it captures average expected tail loss, is sub-additive, and plugs directly into optimisation objectives; the Rolling CVaR Dashboard already in Decifer's roadmap needs a companion hard-budget enforcement gate. The most underappreciated 2024–2025 finding is the structural breakdown of stock-bond correlations (36-month correlation hit +0.66 by December 2024 vs. a 20-year average of –0.10), which means correlation assumptions that underpinned the existing Correlation-Aware Position Management feature are stale and a live, rolling cross-asset correlation monitor with automatic position-size penalties is urgently needed. The convergent theme is that no single sizing method survives regime shifts — the winning architecture combines Bayesian Kelly (self-updating win-rate priors) + volatility-targeting scalar + drawdown-aware reducer + correlation-adjusted portfolio heat cap, operating as composable layers rather than a single formula.
top_3_quick_wins: Bayesian Self-Updating Kelly Sizer — replaces static Kelly with a Beta-distribution win-rate estimator that updates after every closed trade, shrinking toward half-Kelly when sample size is small

Portfolio-Level Volatility Target Enforcer — a single scalar computed as (target_vol / rolling_21d_realised_vol) applied to all new position sizes, preventing over-exposure during calm periods and forced risk reduction during spikes

Drawdown-Proportional Position Scaler — linearly reduces new-trade allocation as current drawdown approaches a configurable max-drawdown threshold, with a concrete Python formula already documented in practitioner literature

findings.expected_impact: Reduces parameter-estimation-driven overbetting; empirical Bayesian shrinkage converges fractional Kelly toward quarter-Kelly on small samples, cutting max drawdown 10–20% vs static Kelly while preserving ~75% of long-run growth rate
findings.summary: Replaces Decifer's static Kelly formula with a Beta-distribution Bayesian updater: after each closed trade, alpha (wins) and beta (losses) are incremented, and the win probability estimate p = alpha/(alpha+beta) feeds the Kelly formula alongside a sample-confidence shrinkage factor (f_adjusted = f_kelly × confidence + 0.25 × (1 - confidence)). This directly addresses the core failure mode documented in multiple 2024–2025 papers — that full Kelly with misestimated inputs leads to catastrophic drawdowns, as MacLean, Ziemba and Blazenko showed empirically. For Decifer specifically, the 9-dimension signal scorer already produces a directional conviction signal that maps naturally onto the p input.
findings.upgrades: Upgrades the existing Kelly criterion position sizing engine with live Bayesian win-rate updating and sample-size-aware shrinkage.
findings.what_changes: Add a BayesianKellyState dataclass with alpha, beta, expected_gain, expected_loss, kelly_fraction fields. After each trade close, update alpha or beta. Compute p = alpha/(alpha+beta), confidence = min(1.0, n_trades/100), f_adjusted = f_kelly * confidence + 0.25 * (1-confidence). Replace the single static Kelly call in position sizing with this rolling estimator. Store state per-symbol or globally depending on config.
findings.why_it_matters: Static Kelly in a paper-trading bot with limited trade history will chronically oversize early on, creating deep drawdowns precisely when the system is being evaluated for live deployment. Bayesian shrinkage solves this automatically without requiring human intervention, and is directly applicable now with zero new data sources.
findings.source_evidence: Medium: 'Bayesian Kelly: A Self-Learning Algorithm for Power Trading' (March 2025, Jonathan Levi) — https://medium.com/@jlevi.nyc/bayesian-kelly-a-self-learning-algorithm-for-power-trading-2e4d7bf8dad6; TradingView Script: 'Kelly Position Size Calculator' (August 2025, EdgeTools) documenting f_adjusted shrinkage formula; investwithcarl.com: 'Dynamic Adaptive Kelly Criterion' (August 2025)

findings.expected_impact: Man Group / Research Affiliates research shows volatility-targeted equity portfolios improve Sharpe ratios vs unscaled portfolios, reduce left-tail severity, and produce smoother equity curves with volatility-of-volatility significantly reduced; PHBS working paper (1990–2023) shows Sharpe improvement from 1.54 to 1.76 for volatility-managed portfolios
findings.summary: Adds a portfolio-level volatility scalar that computes a 21-day exponentially weighted realised volatility estimate for the portfolio's equity curve, then scales all new position sizes by (target_vol / realised_vol), capped at 1.5x leverage. When markets are calm, the multiplier slightly increases exposure; when volatility spikes (as in March 2020 or August 2024), it automatically reduces all new sizing before the next bar. This is distinct from the archived ATR Volatility-Targeting Position Size Layer, which was per-instrument ATR-based — this feature operates at the portfolio equity curve level using exponentially weighted variance. The Springer Nature 2025 paper adds the crucial insight that a rebalancing band (±4%) eliminates unnecessary transaction costs without sacrificing risk control.
findings.upgrades: Adds a portfolio-level volatility normalization layer on top of the existing per-trade position sizing pipeline.
findings.what_changes: Compute daily portfolio returns from the equity curve. Apply exponentially weighted variance (half-life 20 days, matching Harvey et al. methodology) to get sigma_realised. Define target_vol = 0.15 (15% annualised, configurable per user risk profile). Scale = min(target_vol / sigma_realised, MAX_SCALE). Apply Scale to all new position shares before order submission. Implement a ±5% no-rebalance band to avoid churning during small vol changes. Log scale factor to dashboard.
findings.why_it_matters: Decifer currently has no portfolio-level volatility normalization. During low-VIX periods like 2023–2024, the bot would progressively size larger as Kelly fractions look attractive, but when volatility spikes (typical before earnings or macro events), positions are already oversized. This feature provides an automatic mechanical governor that protects paper-trading performance and gives future users a smooth, predictable risk experience.
findings.source_evidence: Man Group: 'The Impact of Volatility Targeting' (Harvey, Hoyle, Korgaonkar et al.) — https://www.man.com/insights/the-impact-of-volatility-targeting; Springer Nature Financial Markets and Portfolio Management (July 2025): 'Target volatility strategies: optimal rebalancing boundary for transaction cost minimization' — https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11408-025-00486-5; Research Affiliates: 'Harnessing Volatility Targeting in Multi-Asset Portfolios' (2024) — https://www.researchaffiliates.com/publications/articles/1014-harnessing-volatility-targeting

findings.expected_impact: Reduces catastrophic loss scenarios by linearly scaling new allocations to zero as drawdown approaches max threshold; practitioner code shows risk_scaling = 1 - (drawdown / max_drawdown), preserving capital during drawdown periods while avoiding the 'locking in losses' problem if max_drawdown threshold is set generously (e.g., 20%)
findings.summary: Implements a simple but powerful drawdown-aware position scaler that multiplies the final position size by risk_scaling = max(0, 1 - (current_drawdown / max_drawdown_threshold)). When the bot is at peak equity, risk_scaling = 1.0 (no reduction). At 10% drawdown with a 20% threshold, risk_scaling = 0.5 (half size). At the threshold, risk_scaling = 0 (no new positions). This is documented with working Python code in published practitioner research and is validated by Chekhlov et al.'s CDaR framework. It directly replaces the archived 'Drawdown Brake — Tiered Equity Curve Circuit Breaker' which used discrete tiers; this uses a continuous scalar that avoids cliff-edge behaviour.
findings.upgrades: Adds a continuous drawdown-proportional multiplier as the final layer in the position sizing pipeline, replacing the archived discrete Drawdown Brake.
findings.what_changes: Track portfolio_peak and current_nav in risk.py. Compute current_drawdown = (portfolio_peak - current_nav) / portfolio_peak. Define max_drawdown_threshold (default 0.20, configurable per user risk profile). risk_scaling = max(0.0, 1.0 - (current_drawdown / max_drawdown_threshold)). Apply risk_scaling as a final multiplier after Kelly + volatility target calculations. Log risk_scaling to dashboard with colour coding (green > 0.8, yellow 0.5–0.8, red < 0.5).
findings.why_it_matters: Paper trading is the stage where user trust is built. A single extended losing streak that blows through 25%+ of paper capital will kill confidence in the system before live trading begins. This scaler ensures the bot self-throttles during adverse periods, which is also exactly the kind of per-account risk control needed for the multi-user SaaS vision — each user's drawdown threshold becomes a configurable risk profile parameter.
findings.source_evidence: Medium: 'Position Sizing Strategies for Algo-Traders' (Jakub Polec, August 2024) with working Python implementation — https://medium.com/@jpolec_72972/position-sizing-strategies-for-algo-traders; SSRN: 'Trade Sizing Techniques for Drawdown and Tail Risk Control' (Strub, Issam) introducing CDaR-based sizing — https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/Delivery.cfm/SSRN_ID3231836; arXiv 2401.02601: 'Constrained Max Drawdown: a Fast and Robust Portfolio Optimization Approach' (January 2024)

findings.feature: Live Cross-Asset Rolling Correlation Decay Alert
findings.expected_impact: Detects correlation regime breaks (stock-bond correlation hit +0.66 in December 2024 vs –0.10 historical average) that make diversification assumptions invalid; prevents position sizing that assumes normal correlation when hedging relationships have broken down, potentially avoiding 15–25% drawdowns experienced in 2022-style regime shifts
findings.summary: Computes rolling 30-day and 90-day pairwise return correlations between all current open positions using yfinance daily returns, and flags when correlation has shifted materially from the baseline (e.g., more than 0.3 absolute change). When the cross-position average correlation exceeds a threshold (e.g., 0.6), a Correlation Surge flag is emitted that reduces new position sizes by a configurable factor. This is distinct from the existing 'Correlation-Aware Position Management' feature, which manages static sector-level correlation caps — this feature specifically monitors the dynamic regime shift in correlations over time and generates alerts when the assumed diversification structure has broken down. The CFA Institute (February 2026) and BlackRock (Fall 2025) research both flag this as the dominant risk management blind spot in current portfolios.
findings.upgrades: Adds a dynamic rolling correlation monitor that operates as a pre-flight check on the existing Correlation-Aware Position Management system.
findings.what_changes: Pull last 90 days of daily close prices for all current open symbols via yfinance. Compute 30d and 90d rolling pairwise correlations. Compute average_correlation = mean of all pairwise correlations. Track baseline_correlation from a 252d lookback. If abs(average_correlation - baseline_correlation) > 0.3 OR average_correlation > 0.65, set correlation_surge = True. When correlation_surge, apply a position_size multiplier of 0.6 to all new orders. Emit a dashboard alert with current vs. baseline correlation values. Recalculate daily at market open.
findings.why_it_matters: The 2022 and 2020 regime breaks both involved sudden correlation flips that standard risk models missed. As Decifer scales to multi-user, friends and family investors will be exposed to periods where the bot's diversification assumptions are invalid. This feature provides a systematic early-warning system grounded in current market data — exactly what static sector caps cannot do.
findings.source_evidence: CFA Institute Enterprising Investor: 'Why Static Portfolios Fail When Risk Regimes Change' (February 2026) — https://blogs.cfainstitute.org/investor/2026/02/20/why-static-portfolios-fail-when-risk-regimes-change/; BlackRock: '2025 Fall Investment Directions' noting 36-month stock-bond correlation spiked to 0.66 — https://www.blackrock.com/us/financial-professionals/insights/investment-directions-fall-2025; PGIM: 'Cross-Asset Correlations in Market Turbulence' (2024) — https://www.pgim.com

findings.expected_impact: Aligns Kelly fraction directly with composite signal conviction (score 0–100 mapped to a 0.25x–1.0x Kelly multiplier), expected to improve risk-adjusted returns by concentrating risk on highest-conviction trades; practitioner Bayesian framework shows integrating adaptive Kelly with ensemble signals 'can yield superior portfolio performance'
findings.summary: Modifies the Kelly position sizing to multiply the fractional Kelly output by a signal-strength scalar derived from Decifer's existing 9-dimension composite score. A score of 90+ maps to kelly_multiplier = 1.0 (full fractional Kelly), a score of 70 maps to 0.6, and a score below the consensus threshold maps to 0.0 (no trade). This directly operationalises the Bayesian Kelly principle that bet size should track edge conviction, and connects the signal scoring subsystem directly to the risk engine in a principled way. The formula is: final_size = base_kelly * signal_multiplier(score), where signal_multiplier = (score - min_threshold) / (100 - min_threshold) clamped to [0.25, 1.0].
findings.upgrades: Creates a direct mathematical bridge between the signal scoring engine (signals.py) and the Kelly position sizing in risk.py.
findings.what_changes: Accept composite_score (0–100) as an input to the position sizing function. Define MIN_THRESHOLD = consensus_threshold (from existing config). Compute signal_multiplier = clamp((composite_score - MIN_THRESHOLD) / (100 - MIN_THRESHOLD), 0.25, 1.0). Multiply final Kelly fraction by signal_multiplier before share quantity calculation. Log signal_multiplier alongside position size in the trade journal so it can be analysed for IC correlation in the Alpha Decay system.
findings.why_it_matters: Currently Decifer treats all trades above the consensus threshold equally from a sizing perspective. A score of 72 gets the same Kelly fraction as a score of 95. This is the single easiest way to extract more value from the existing signal infrastructure without adding any new signals — high-conviction trades carry more weight, low-conviction trades carry less.
findings.source_evidence: investwithcarl.com: 'Dynamic Adaptive Kelly Criterion' (August 2025) — 'Integrating adaptive Kelly strategies with traditional risk management frameworks and ensemble learning methods can yield superior portfolio performance'; arXiv 2508.16598: 'Sizing the Risk: Kelly, VIX, and Hybrid Approaches' (August 2025) — hybrid sizing method 'consistently balances return generation with robust drawdown control'

findings.feature: Rolling CVaR Position Budget Enforcement Gate
findings.expected_impact: Provides a mathematically coherent tail-risk cap that prevents new positions from being opened when the portfolio's expected tail loss (CVaR@95) exceeds a configurable budget; CVaR is sub-additive and captures average loss beyond VaR, making it 10–30% more conservative than VaR during stress periods as empirically documented in 2024–2025 research
findings.summary: Computes the portfolio's rolling 95% CVaR using historical simulation over the trailing 60 trading days of equity curve returns. CVaR@95 = average of the worst 5% of daily portfolio returns. If CVaR@95 exceeds a per-user budget (e.g., –2.5% expected daily loss in the tail), new position opens are blocked until the metric recovers. This is distinct from the existing 'Rolling CVaR Dashboard Metric and Per-Account Risk Budget' (which is analytics-only) by adding an actual enforcement gate that prevents order submission. VaR cannot fill this role because it ignores losses beyond the threshold and is not sub-additive across positions.
findings.upgrades: Adds a hard enforcement gate to the existing Rolling CVaR Dashboard Metric, converting it from a display metric into an active risk limiter.
findings.what_changes: Pull last 60 trading days of portfolio daily returns from the trade journal. Sort returns. CVaR_95 = mean(worst 3 days out of 60, i.e. bottom 5%). Compare CVaR_95 against cvar_budget (configurable, default –0.025). If CVaR_95 < cvar_budget (i.e., tail losses too large), set new_position_allowed = False. Log CVaR_95 to existing Rolling CVaR Dashboard panel. When blocked, emit a user notification explaining the gate. Gate resets automatically when CVaR recovers above threshold.
findings.why_it_matters: The existing Rolling CVaR Dashboard shows the number but does nothing with it. For multi-user SaaS, each user's cvar_budget becomes a first-class risk profile parameter. A conservative user (retirement savings) gets cvar_budget = –0.015; an aggressive user gets –0.04. This turns the dashboard metric into actual protection.
findings.source_evidence: ScienceDirect: 'Portfolio performance evaluation in Mean-CVaR framework' demonstrating CVaR outperforms VaR as coherent risk measure; IAENG Int. J. Applied Mathematics: 'CVaR-based Portfolio with Investment Views Given by LLMs' (2025) — 'CVaR@95 portfolio based on Claude achieves best risk and return indicators such as SR and CR'; Management Science: 'A Comparison of VaR and CVaR Constraints on Portfolio Selection' — 'CVaR constraint is more effective than VaR as a tool to control risk'

findings.feature: Conditional Drawdown at Risk (CDaR) Trade Sizer
findings.expected_impact: Applies Extreme Value Theory to the drawdown distribution rather than the returns distribution, computing a smooth leverage factor based on CDaR that produces 'a Sharpe ratio slightly higher than for the original strategies' and evolves smoothly to avoid high transaction costs — documented in Strub's SSRN paper across 10 years of live trading data
findings.summary: Implements the CDaR-based trade sizing algorithm from Strub (SSRN): instead of using return volatility to size positions, it applies Extreme Value Theory (EVT) to fit a generalised Pareto distribution to the tail of the historical drawdown distribution, and uses CDaR (mean of worst-alpha drawdowns) as the sizing denominator. This produces a leverage factor that is smooth, adapts to changing drawdown risk, and is more robust to fat-tailed return distributions than simple vol-scaling. The CDaR approach is distinct from all existing features because it operates on the drawdown time-series directly rather than on returns or volatility.
findings.upgrades: Introduces a drawdown-distribution-based sizing algorithm as an optional alternative to standard Kelly, providing a more sophisticated tail-risk-aware sizing approach.
findings.what_changes: Compute running drawdown series from portfolio equity curve. Fit a generalised Pareto distribution (GPD) to the tail using scipy.stats.genpareto. Compute CDaR_alpha = E[drawdown | drawdown > VaR_alpha of drawdowns]. Define CDaR_target (configurable, e.g., 0.10 = 10% CDaR budget). CDaR_leverage = CDaR_target / CDaR_alpha. Apply CDaR_leverage as a scalar on all position sizes. Fall back to 1.0 scalar if insufficient drawdown history (< 30 observations). This requires scipy (free) and standard yfinance history.
findings.why_it_matters: For Decifer's transition from paper to live, the most dangerous scenario is an unexpected sustained drawdown that erodes user confidence. CDaR sizing explicitly targets and controls this, producing position sizes that respond to the actual drawdown experience rather than return volatility alone — a more direct alignment with what users actually care about.
findings.source_evidence: SSRN / ResearchGate: 'Trade Sizing Techniques for Drawdown and Tail Risk Control' (Strub, Issam) — 'CDaR-based algorithm: Sharpe ratio slightly higher, leverage factor smooth, less likely to suffer from high transaction costs' — https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/Delivery.cfm/SSRN_ID3231836; arXiv 2401.02601: 'Constrained Max Drawdown: a Fast and Robust Portfolio Optimization Approach' (January 2024)

findings.feature: Adaptive Tail-Risk Stress Scenario Pre-Trade Gate
findings.expected_impact: Before opening a new position, simulates the portfolio's exposure to three standardised stress scenarios (equity –20%, VIX +100%, correlation flip to +0.8); blocks or reduces trades that would push scenario loss beyond per-user budget; Resonanz Capital framework shows institutional allocators dedicate 0.5–1% NAV to structured hedging once this diagnostic is in place
findings.summary: Implements a lightweight pre-trade stress test that applies three shock scenarios to the proposed new position and existing portfolio before order submission. If any scenario produces a simulated loss exceeding the user's stress_loss_budget (e.g., –15% in a single shock), the trade size is reduced proportionally. This is not Monte Carlo (which exists in the backlog) but rather a fast deterministic scenario engine using configurable shock vectors. It operationalises the 'Diagnose Vulnerabilities' step from Resonanz Capital's institutional tail-hedging framework (July 2025), making it accessible for retail users without requiring options or derivatives.
findings.upgrades: Adds a pre-trade deterministic stress scenario check as a final gate before order is submitted to the execution layer.
findings.what_changes: Define three stress scenarios as configuration: EQUITY_CRASH = {'spy': -0.20, 'default_beta': -0.25}, VIX_SPIKE = {'spy': -0.10, 'high_beta': -0.18}, CORRELATION_FLIP = {'corr_adjustment': +0.5}. For each proposed trade, compute stressed_portfolio_loss for each scenario by applying shocks to current + proposed positions using yfinance betas. If max(stressed_losses) > stress_loss_budget, reduce proposed size by the ratio (stress_loss_budget / stressed_loss). Log which scenario triggered the reduction. Fast enough to run synchronously at order generation time.
findings.why_it_matters: When Decifer moves to multi-user SaaS with friends-and-family investors, a single black-swan drawdown will destroy trust and user retention. This gate provides a visible, explainable risk control that users can see in the dashboard ('Position reduced by 40% due to tail-risk stress check'), directly supporting the trust-building narrative needed before live capital is committed.
findings.source_evidence: Resonanz Capital: 'Strategic Tail-Risk Hedging: Building Antifragility into Institutional Portfolios' (July 2025) — stress-test framework: 'equity –30%, credit-spread +400bp, bond-equity correlation +1 scenarios' — https://resonanzcapital.com/insights/strategic-tail-risk-hedging; QuantPedia: 'Hedging Tail Risk with Robust VIXY Models' (October 2025) — conditional VIX-signal tail hedge implementation — https://quantpedia.com/hedging-tail-risk-with-robust-vixy-models/
